items.title: Polynomial degree and unstable fits
items.scenario: You fit degree-1, degree-3, and degree-15 polynomials to 20 noisy points from a smooth underlying curve. Each time you resample 20 points and refit, the degree-15 curve wiggles wildly through different noise patterns.
items.explanation: The high-degree model has high variance: E_D[ŷ(x)] may still be reasonable at some x, but individual fits scatter far from that mean. Degree-1 is stable (low variance) but may miss curvature (high bias). Degree-3 often balances both on this small sample.

items.title: Deep decision tree on tabular data
items.scenario: A fraud team trains a decision tree with max_depth=None on 500 labeled transactions. Training accuracy is 99%; validation on next month's data is 82%. Retraining on a different 500-row sample changes which rules fire at the top of the tree.
items.explanation: Unrestricted depth lets the tree memorize idiosyncratic rows—classic high variance. Predictions depend heavily on which rare fraud patterns appeared in training. Pruning or limiting depth reduces variance at the cost of some training accuracy.

items.title: k-NN with k=1 vs k=15
items.scenario: For the same 2D classification task, k=1 always labels a point by its single nearest neighbor; k=15 votes among 15 neighbors.
items.explanation: k=1 has high variance: moving one training point can flip a prediction boundary locally. k=15 averages over more neighbors, smoothing decision boundaries and lowering variance, but may blur fine structure (higher bias).

items.title: Bagging random forests vs a single tree
items.scenario: An engineer compares one deep tree to a random forest of 200 bootstrapped trees on the same dataset.
items.explanation: Each tree has high variance alone; averaging many decorrelated tree predictions reduces Var(ŷ) while retaining expressive power. The forest's validation error often drops because variance shrinks faster than bias rises.
